title: "Why so vehement? So unyielding? So severe?"
author: Sam Riley
orientation: Sam Riley defends a deliberately vehement, uncompromising rhetorical posture against abortion by appealing to William Lloyd Garrison and biblical precedents, arguing that an entrenched culture of death cannot be melted by gentle persuasion alone but only by sustained, principled heat aimed at immediate abolition.
audiences: Pro-life advocates uncomfortable with abolitionist tone
Christians weighing strategy and evangelism style
Historians of antislavery and reform movements
Critics who see abolitionists as needlessly divisive

questions.question: Why does the author insist that vehement, severe rhetoric is necessary rather than gentle persuasion?
questions.audience: Pro-life advocates uncomfortable with abolitionist tone
questions.answer: Riley argues that the entrenched culture of death is so massive and frozen that only sustained heat can dissolve it, quoting Garrison's metaphor of an iceberg larger than any in the arctic. Soft persuasion may suit the willing listener, but for those who persist in evil, heat is required.
questions.quote: There is a place for speaking gently to those who are willing to listen, but to those who won't listen who persist in their evil, heat is necessary.

questions.question: How does the author justify mocking and ridiculing evil rather than only reasoning with it?
questions.audience: Christians weighing strategy and evangelism style
questions.answer: He grounds the practice in God's own mockery of pagan idols in Scripture, citing Psalm 135 and Isaiah 46, and argues that idols stop looking beautiful once they are exposed as empty and pathetic. Verbal assault on idols is presented as a form of liberating those who cling to them.
questions.quote: How do you dislodge idols from the hands of those who love them? You assail them verbally and make them look as empty, evil, and pathetic as they truly are. You expose idolaters as creatures of the dark and call them to the light.

questions.question: What model of historical reform does the article hold up, and what does it reject?
questions.audience: Historians of antislavery and reform movements
questions.answer: Riley holds up reformers who refused to compromise truth for coalition-building and sought infamy and division rather than popularity. He rejects the modern instinct to soften principle in order to assemble broad alliances, arguing past reformers drew sharp lines and demanded purity.
questions.quote: Reformers of the past didn't compromise truth to coalition build; they didn't seek popularity, they sought infamy and division. They set themselves apart from their culture and drew lines in the sand.

questions.question: What biblical figures are offered as examples of the dangerous heat the author commends?
questions.audience: Christians weighing strategy and evangelism style
questions.answer: He names Stephen, whose witness brought stoning, and John the Baptist, whose rebuke of Herod cost him his head, alongside Garrison's printing press as a modern parallel. The pattern is that uncompromising speech invites violent reaction but ultimately topples evil regimes.
questions.quote: It was the brilliant heat that Stephen gave off that got his skull crushed by hundreds of stones, it was the heat off of John the Baptist's tongue that burned Herod and got John's head chopped off, it was the heat off of Garrison's press that brought the slave nation to its knees, and it will be the heat of a fiery sword that slays the abortion dragon of the West.

questions.question: What is the stated goal of abolitionist agitation, according to the article?
questions.answer: The aim is not merely to disturb or pressure the existing system but to destroy it entirely. Persistent attack, not the popularizing of life-affirming alternatives, is presented as the mechanism by which the culture of death is killed.
questions.quote: The goal is not to merely rock the boat but to sink the ship. The culture of death dies by persistent attack, not by popularizing life.

questions.question: How does Riley extend Garrison's critique from southern slaveholders to northern allies?
questions.audience: Historians of antislavery and reform movements
questions.answer: He notes Garrison attacked not only the slave state of the South but the racism of northerners who professed to oppose slavery while benefiting from the system and dehumanizing slaves through their own efforts. The parallel implied is that today's pro-life establishment can profess opposition to abortion while perpetuating it.
questions.quote: The attacks he leveled not only against the slave state of the South but the racism of northerners who benefited from the system while simultaneously purporting to oppose it with show bills and efforts that dehumanized the slave.

questions.question: Why does the article reject incremental pro-life bills as inconsistent with being "all on fire"?
questions.audience: Pro-life advocates uncomfortable with abolitionist tone
questions.answer: Riley argues that proposing legislation which sacrifices some children to save others uses the same altar of judicial supremacy as the abortion industry itself. Only immediate abolition matches the consistency of God's love and avoids being a stench before Him.
questions.quote: Immediate, not incremental, abolition is the only standard we can adopt if it be truly principled. You cannot be "all on fire" as Garrison while proposing bills that sacrifice some children to save others, burning them on the same altar of judicial supremacy the world burns them on.

questions.question: What does the author identify as the core reason the corrupt status quo persists?
questions.audience: Critics who see abolitionists as needlessly divisive
questions.answer: He claims that comfort and stability are the human default, while change feels chaotic and uncertain, so people instinctively defend whatever they already know. This frames the abolitionist's vehemence as the necessary counterweight to inertia, not gratuitous aggression.
questions.quote: The abortion-loving, pornified, plastic status quo exists because people love the status quo. The norm is comfort and stability. Change is chaotic and uncertain. People are sure to cling to what they know.

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
related.reason: Develops the immediatist principle that this article invokes when rejecting incremental bills.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Provides the substantive critique of incrementalism that Riley's rhetorical argument presupposes.

related.title: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism in a Nutshell
related.reason: Compact framing of the same immediate-vs-gradual divide that animates this piece's closing call.

related.title: King Josiah's Smashmouth Abolitionism
related.reason: Offers a biblical model of the uncompromising, idol-smashing reform posture Riley commends.

related.title: The Holiness of God Destroys Pro-Life Incrementalism
related.reason: Reinforces the theological grounding for the consistency Riley demands at the article's end.

related.title: Four Problems With David Barton's Incrementalism Apologia
related.reason: Engages a defender of incrementalism using the same Garrison-derived antislavery framework.

related.title: Why Do Pro-Lifers Despise Abolitionists?
related.reason: Addresses the hostile reaction to abolitionist tone that Riley's defense of vehemence anticipates.
